text: Nice environment, reasonable food, and attentive service...but...There were no nibbles of any sort available at the (pleasant) upstairs bar with drinks.The garlic prawns were tasty with a rich creamy sauce, but came with only 3 wafer thin slices of baguette to mop up the sauce. We got more when we asked, but why ration bread in such an expensive restaurant?Pomfret with tandoori was an interesting concept. Fish was well cooked but not sure in the end if we were eating tandoori sauce or tom yum sauce.Veges were fresh and not overcoked.The CRAB??? Everyone says to have the crab. Credit to chef for a different take on chilli crab, and it was nice enough, but again not something that I would return for. Chilli was pretty bland. Try it once if you have to.We objected to the continual sell - eg the waiter was insistent we have the most expensive wine on the menu, which happened to be a big Australian red, and not appropriate for seafood. We opted for the NZ Pinot Gris was fine. We were also told we needed to order a larger crab, more dishes, etc etc. Then there was the push by several of the staff to ensure we gave them a good TA review. As for the manager lighting up a cigarette beside us while we were still eating, then going off and leaving it smouldering in the ashtray? I don't think I will bother with this place again, which is a shame because it has potential.
label: neutral

text: My husband and I dined here this evening. Not easy to find but from the moment we walked in we both loved it. Lighting which is often overlooked in restaurants was lovely, soft and ambient. We had some lovely cocktails to start followed by chicken skewers, very tasty. We were seated under a large fan and found it to be quite cold, our issue was dealt with immediately by the staff and the ceiling fan was turned down to low. I had the vegetarian pizza made with feta from Borneo,loved it. My husband had lamb mousakka which he enjoyed, he had cheesecake for dessert while I ordered the much acclaimed chilli chocolate mousse with vanilla ice cream. Due to the strong punchy flavours of the chilli this desert was very filling-about right for two people sharing with I would suggest an extra scoop of ice cream.I could only manage to finish half. The owner is very personable and chatted to us,even though there were two large parties dining there,including the French ambassadors party! The owner was not aware that I worked as a food critic for many years and I can honestly say this is the best food in Kuching,
label: positive

text: I've patronised Jln Alor during my past 3 visits to KL and have always had great experience w the food there. This time round, the unit which I usually patronise is closed, hence I thought i should try other restaurants which should be ok. I went into the 3rd unit 鑫記, which displayed some pretty nice photos of food and some dishes which I was looking for. This has turned out to be my worst dinner, ever, in KL. I ordered 3 dishes: Vinegar Pork Trotter, Sambal Clams and Stir Fried Kailan. Vinegar pork: sticky, gluey, slimy sauce, totally no vinegar taste. Pork was cold, though the claypot was hot. It tasted terrible. Horrendous.Sambal Clams: clams were not fresh, all the clam meat dropped out from shell when they serve. They put in more lady's finger than clams in the dish. Speechless. Vegetable: veg was yellowish w lots of black spots. Took a closer look and found a lot of tiny black worms on vegetable!! We left the place with the food almost untouched. Guess how much was the damage? RM 59!!Pls dun patronise this stall! Hopefully, my regular stall will open tonight. I still miss the good food there.

text: Before i begin, this review may be biased, and may be a matter of perspective and preferance.Anyways i went for lunch at Yeast with a friend of mine recently after hearing good reviews of the restaurant. The duck dishes are apparently good so i gave it a try. I ordered the duck confit and my friend had a vegetarian sandwich with cheese. I've had duck confit in France a few years ago so i was able to compare both the dish. I must say that i was quite disappointed at what was on my plate as the meat was tough and rather dry as opposed to what i've tasted abroad. The dish comes with salad and i feel that there was no effort put into preparing the greens cause there was no dressing at all. But i must say the cappuccino was good and i suppose that's a consolation to my not so satisfying meal.I guess people have different expectations when it comes to food and i may be a bit more fussy than the others. But all in all, the service is a pleasant one .

text: We returned here after a pleasant lunch of Bouillabaisse and a salmon lunch special a few weeks back. Both dishes were very well prepared, and we were especially keen to order the Bouillabaisse again. I haven't seen this dish in Asia before and this rendition was nicely prepared and presented. The Rouille was tasty and sour dough bread perfectly toasted/grilled. The Bouillabaisse included prawns, fish, muscles and clams and a nice broth. Some of the other mains looked quite inviting as well. We also tried the salad Italiana which looked rather simple but actually tasted good especially the dried tomatoes. We finished up with some macaroons which were ok at best and an iced latte that was devine. Given our cake was pretty bad on our first visit, I would say deserts are not their forte. Please note it can get loud in this location with many noisy restaurants and lousy surrounding acoustics which makes imagining you are sitting at a noisy Parisan street side cafe a piece of cake -;

text: Had our first meal in Langkawi here due to its high ratings. Arrived at 7pm on a weekday but we were the only customers here. More customers arrived around 7.30pm. Ordered the Hotplate seafood for RM$60, thinking that the 3 of us could share but to our surprise, there were 5 prawns, 1 scallop, 2 mussels, 1-2 squids and some rice - all for a staggering RM$60 .... Frankly, this price is still quite acceptable in a Singapore Korean restaurant but come on, this is Malaysia. With the price that this Restaurant charges, its prices are even higher than nearby Penang where we visited twice recently, in fact, much much higher! Next, the side dish of seafood pancake was reasonably priced and portions acceptable but we hardly find any visible seafood in the pancake ....After we complained about the RM$60 main course, we were served complimentary plate of vermicelli with vegetables - a token from the nice owners, who are undoubtedly friendly, humble and very engaging. But the bill for the 3 of us came up to more than RM$100 (add another 10% service charge), and it has become the least value for money meal for us in a 6 days Langkawi trip. We later had real seafood meals in Langkawi and almost all costs less than this first Korean meal.

text: It was recommended by a friend. We went there for lunch as we wanted to try something new and to our surprise, the food didn't disappoint. We had noodle soup and pork buns, which were delicious, and the food came fast although the place was full. Food wise, we look forward to going back to try other reputable selections, not for lunch though as the queue can be rather long.In the afternoon, the atmosphere is what you'd expect from a local restaurant that has appeared several times in the newspaper. It's fully packed! You'll have to arrive early to get a seat, earlier if you come in a large group. If you were to come a group of 2-3, expect to be accommodated in a table with others. This might be a deal breaker to some but I had a good experience doing so. I befriended two friendly bankers who were there for the famous noodle soup. If you're interested in their menu, you may try to google it to find out what excites you. The waiters/waitresses there are friendly and attentive to the clients. They were fast and polite in their responses when you request for stuffs like extra plate or utensils. Most importantly, they are clean!!! Overall, I had a good time eating there and look forward to going to their new place once its open for business. Enjoy! ^^

text: I am Chinese, and hence my standard of Chinese food is unmistakably high. We have been to Beijing's famous "Da dong" for Peking duck a few times, and whilst we were not expecting Da dong standards, we were certainly surprised at the lifeless, somewhat soggy duck that came out of the kitchen. At first I thought it was just the colour- it must taste good. After all, this is a five star shangri-la hotel, right? The waitress was polite, and wrapped up the duck in the skin for us, which was nice. However, a few bites had proven my instincts right. The duck skin was greasy and soggy, not crispy at all! There was also a strange musk that my mom disliked, a lot. We could not force ourselves to eat it, and mentioned this to the waitress who seemed a bit embarrassed, and also agreed the skin "isn't so crisp today". Thankfully when I spoke to the manager (whom I had to catch the attention of, and not initiated by our lovely waitress), he was apologetic, and cancelled our order completely. We had a brief conversation what standards I was comparing to. I don't want to be unfair, but I have also sampled Peking duck in Singapore and Hong Kong, and has expected somewhat similar standards. I think it is a little ambitious to have such an extensive menu, and include something as difficult as Peking duck. It is difficult to be done properly. The sweet and sour pork we ordered was..... Just about ok. Not to my taste- there was still some sauce on the plate, which does not happen if in HK restaurants. All sauces would be stuck onto the meat itself, and not leaving a slightly watery fluid at the bottom of the plate. The pork was also cut into very thin strips, like the UK style takeaway crispy chilli beef, which I could only give them the benefit of hither doubt, that they are catering for foreigners! All the authentic sweet and sour pork I have grown up eating, are generous chunks of meat, not strips of 70% batter. The "E Fu" noodles we ordered were again not typical- it is the healthier, non fried type. Which is better for my health I suppose! But if you order this dish in a Cantonese restaurant, you will get thick strips of noodles (admittedly deep fried). The noodles taste good nonetheless. The best dish was our only vegetarian main- bitter gourd with tofu! It was up to scratch, and compliments were due. The staff were attentive and polite, even after I raised issues with the duck. They came to check that the rest of the dishes were ok, which I appreciated. I gave it two stars, not because the food is so horrendous- but because I expected so much more from an in house restaurant based in a reputable five star hotel, in the capital of Malaysia. You may get lucky and have a good experience, and the dishes on some other tables certainly looked ok. However, as a five star hotel restaurant, all food should be of a consistently excellent standard.
label: negative

text: Neroteca is located in a nice location with plenty of parking. Surrounded by residential area gives a sense of space.Not too many tables and booking is advised. Set up is pretty cozy having both tables and booth. Service was casual and yet efficient. You do not have the feeling that they just want to get your order and out you go like some restaurants.Menu is pretty complete – most of what u can think of when it comes to Italian. We ordered a lasagna and sea food pasta accompanied with some red wine. While waiting to be served, I was lucky enough to be seated next to a window with a bird’s eye view of the kitchen. Few things happening in the kitchen did not impress me. Kitchen helper sawing a T-bone into serving portions likely to be frozen. Throughout the dinner about 1.5 hours, he was interrupted with several other task and the cutting was still not completed when I left. Not the best way to treat a piece of good meat. Shell fish was from a frozen pack. Given the menu price, I would expect live or at least something from the fish market. I’m sure Bens would have them. Lasagna was OK but the dough was on the thicker side. Would have been nice if there was a little more meat and cheese. Seafood pasta was not great either. Seafood did not have the “fresh” taste or feeling. Overall service – 5, pricing – 3, ambience – 4, food – 3.

We had an eel maki roll at 50 ++ it was almost twice price of a similar dish we had at sushi Tei and was no better. text: This is my first time visit and the service was terrible. Table was full, so we were agreed to enjoy the bar while waiting the reservation, we ordered some drinks and finally we got our table. We ordered some food and dessert also another drinks to a waiter called "MUGI". To be honest, their service are bad, everything took long time, min 20 min for a beer. FTI, the live band play at 11.00 pm.While we enjoying our time there, 20 min before 11pm, Mugi came and asked us to move coz the table is reserved. The person which booked the table will open 2 bottles. That time we're still ordering some drinks... we told him that nobody tells us this table is reserved and our bill is more than 2 bottles cost. Our neighbours only have 1 beer in their table, some only dessert, why dont he ask them to move while we spent more than RM 300. He was quiet atm, didnt disturb us anymore till before 12 he came again and told me that the person is there already. I was so upset and cannot even enjoy my last beer and the music. In deepest heart, i could tolerate coz he just do his job but he should know also that be professional is a must. I will never ever back there again. Food, music and live band just average. Nothing special except the service. Was really good experienced for us recall we always had nice time in other Hard Rock Cafe.

text: Finally decided to see what the hype was about Yellow Brick Road and Wicked Pancake Parlour.We went as a family of 3 (including our 11-month old) on a Saturday morning and by 10am there was a queue outside the restaurant. As it was quite hazy and I did not see a wait staff from the outside, I walked in to ask if we were going to have to wait long. A waitress came up to me and without even so much of a 'Hello', she ordered me to go back outside as there was quite a queue and promptly left. We walked around the block to see if there was another cafe we could go to but realised everything was shut. Since we were quite hungry, we went back to YBR again and this time were greeted by 2 other staff who promptly showed us upstairs (WPP) where we realised was only half full!We had sat for a full 5 minutes before we were given menus and we asked for a baby chair twice. On the second time a waiter told us that they would bring one to us as soon as they had one available. About 20 minutes later another waitress brought us our order and I asked for a baby chair again, to which she replied there weren't any. Husband walked downstairs and found 3 baby chairs available and brought one up himself. All this was done in the full view of the waiter and waitress who told us that there were none available and neither of them came up to apologise or explain why they couldn't bring one to us in the first place.Baby threw up mid-way of our meal and we decided to clean her up and leave. We explained and apologise to the wait staff about the mess on the floor (I tried to clean up as much puke off the baby chair as possible). Waiter nodded and gave us this grumpy and annoyed look. In fact, every staff we met seemed unhappy and grumpy.The food in itself is average. Poached eggs were overcooked (yolks were almost solid) and the toast was soggy. The coffees had a slightly burnt taste.Overall the experience was anything but frustrating; and we definitely will not be back.
label: negative

text: For a restaurant with enough seats for up to 30 (based on the number of chairs they laid out), I expected the food to be amazing. When I saw that most of the very good reviews on Tripadvisor, I walked in. For starters, the staff didn't seem too eager to have me and my friend for dinner - we were the only two persons there on a Sunday evening. Two other persons dropped in after awhile. So with just 4 of us, I thought perhaps the staff would engage in some conversation to recommend us some signature dishes there. They didn't even bother, instead they were looking out at the street half dreaming. I ordered fried chicken with Balinese herbs with yellow rice and some veges as side dishes. My friend had her grilled pork ribs set. Sadly, her (overly cooked) set which was served together with mine was cool instead of hot. It's as though they left her set while mine was being prepared so both sets could be served together. My fried chicken was very nicely done and I especially enjoyed the refreshing lemongrass drink too. I would come back but I don't think this place deserves the #4 spot in PJ (based on whatever ratings sent in by reviewers). Price-wise, this place isn't too pricey.
label: neutral

text: Went for dinner at 6pm on a Sunday but after getting there found out it didn't open until 6.30. We were happy to wait so went downstairs and had a nice drink in the lobby lounge, which was very nice. Ended up getting to the restaurant around 6.45 and were the only customers there. We were shown a table and asked for drink orders. My husband asked for a vodka drink and the waiter said " I don't know... Ask my captain." Then walked off. I got my tea and they poured some for my husband too but he doesn't drink tea (this is relevant because they charged us for 2 teas when they served out of the one pot which they kept on the table.) We ordered two beef dishes and a rice dish... Only one beef dish and the rice came out. Trying to explain we ordered another beef dish to the waitress was beyond difficult, so we ended up saying don't worry about it.There was lots of awkwardness serving our food - serving me my rice in a bowl and beef on my plate... But serving nothing to my husband. They didn't even get him a bowl... Until he had already served the food himself onto a plate.It felt like there were too many wait staff hovering over us, refilling my tea after almost every sip, and offering to serve more of my husbands rice onto his plate after he had already started doing it himself. One waiter came and took away my bowl and some plates before we had actually finished, and later a waitress offered to pack the food as take away ... While my husband was still eating.Thankfully the food was very good, and we liked the design and decor of the restaurant, unfortunately the awkward and unnecessary level of (not-so-great) service was too notable to allow us to give a better rating.Perhaps go when it's a bit busier and they have more people to focus on?

text: We returned to Sirroco restaurant after half a year (two weeks back) and can I just say it still is living up to standards. Truly one of our most favourite Italian restaurants.The service as usual was impeccable Many thanks to Jamal (he still remebered us) who made our dinner pleasant and enjoyable. He is a rare gem to find in the F&B these days! Food here generally is still tasty as usual even though the menu has changed since our last visit. We had the Minestrone; Mushroom soup; Aglio olio (with beef); Alla sirroco and the Quattro stagioni pizza.Just some memorable mentions.The Alla sirroco was extraordinary. It is a seafood special with crab, fish scallops and more. There is a hint of lemon and tangy taste. A pasta that lives up to its namesake.For the pizza, a meat lover's delight, ingedients included smoke duck, beef and chicken pepperoni. As usual the crust was the highlight. Simply oven-burnt to perfection and authentic.However regrettably... the aglio olio, once the highlight now the bane of the meal. It was dry - literally oven baked. Not a hint of olive oil. The change in menu resulted in this disappointment... The only good thing was that they were generous with beef slices. So please, I implore the restaurant to change it back to the old menu. All in all we still had a wonderful experience here at the Sirroco. Apart from the aglio olio letdown, it was still a lovely dinner, so expect us to return soon!
label: positive

text: I am today years old when I ate the best lamb shank. The meat literally falls off the bone, so tender and beautifully cooked and seasoned to perfection, the mashed potato with the perfect texture, beans and carrots add the perfect sweetness and crunch along with the savoury rosemary sauce. The mushroom and pumpkin soup, perfecto! Don’t get me started with the fluffy and beautiful garlic bread. The pesto bread, topped with melted mozzarella and Parmesan cheese, divine. My friends have tried the beef lasagna, Angus Beef Rib Eye Fettuccine and beef burgers, and they couldn’t stop mentioning how amazing the food is with every bite. The owner, Neville Jacob, is a humble and amazing chef. Definitely this place is an underrated gem. Will definitely be celebrating all my upcoming birthdays here.
label: positive

Our group picked some local and western meal of Pineapple fried rice, fettucine carbonara, nasi lemak and mushroom soup. The soup came to us cold and also the rice. I suspect the carbonara was made with milk base instead of cream and when I asked for cheese, instead of pamesan, I was given cream cheese. We sent back the plastic looking eggs (looking as though they had been left over from breakfast) that came with the Malaysian dishes and got some replacement eggs.The overall experience was miserable and the prices charged were the regular Hard Rock Cafe charges, only that we did not receive the expected meal.

text: We go regularly to Leonardo’s, for years now. It’s a bit tricky to find as you need to locate the door between the restaurants on the ground floor in this street. But once you’re there and climbed the stairs, I suggest you keep climbing to the 2nd floor. It has the same menu iirc, though the 2nd floor has cosy seating arrangements which change the atmosphere. The 1st floor is more formal. The menu changed recently and some great dishes are no more so 1 star less. However, instead there’s now ample choice of cold cuts, including the famous iberico ham which they’ll cut right next to you. I recommend the meatballs (comes with mashed potatoes and great sauce) and a few cold cuts. Combine this with the house wine and you’re set. A perfect place for a relaxed evening out for couples or small groups of friends.

text: Tried this restaurant during my stay in MO in July, was looking out for the Sze Chuan cuisine show-case. Items we ordered includes Soup of the Day and Clear chicken soup, Sze Chuan Mapo tofu, Sze Chuan Spicy diced chilli chicken (La Zi Ji). Both soups are fine, but I would expect more usage of dried ingredients apart from Conpoy, as cantonese cuisines generally have generous amount of dried seafood to season the boiled soup. The soups ordered felt rather lackluster, bland. The biggest disappointments come from the Sze Chuan cuisines which was the highlight of the month. Being a Szechuan cuisine lover and frequent tasting of Sze Chuan restaurants (in Chengdu, Beijing, Harbin and Singapore), the dishes in Lai Po Heen are totally off the taste. Mapo Tofu - is nothing but just spicy sweet and sour tofu. Being a high-end restaurant, using the wrong chilli for the dish is not forgivable. Chilli padi is way too strong, it masked totally the small amount of green peppercorn used to prepare the cuisine. The sauce is way too sweet, it's more like a fusion of peranakan styled tofu cubes. Perhaps the chef should visit Chen Mapo in Chengdu before offering this on the menu; and proper sourcing of chili (dried). Even if making your own fusion, it shouldn't be chili padi sweet tofu. SC La Zi ji - there's an identity crisis. Yes the fried dried chili are there, but the chickens are cut in such big chunks, and i supposed either honey glazed or caramelized, it taste almost like Gongbao Chicken instead of La Zi Ji. Again there's a lack of sufficient peppercorns used, which can be tasted as it lacks the aroma from the peppercorn. The sweetness is overdone. Quality of the chili used is questionable, either its masked by chili padi or it's not fried to the crunchiness to extract the flavors out. Overall, the sze chuan cuisines felt like a fusion of malay and chinese dishes; nothing sze chuan at all apart from the names. You're better off to stick with the original menu of cantonese food. P.S.: I ordered one Mango Pomelo Sago, it tasted more like mango milk. Too much cream, there's no mango freshness. Your chef should probably just try out the mango dessert from Hui Lau Shan next door at Suria KLCC. I don't know seriously how can you serve such a off-tasting famous mango dessert...
label: negative

text: We were lucky to be in town for a Bossa Nova performance by Samba Selamba. Lead vocalist, Viv Adram, has the pipes for more than the conventional airy-fairy, Astrud Gilberto-esque style of singing and when done right, made me wish the song would never end. She was firmly supported by the equally talented Az Samad and Arif Yusof. Arif was amazingly proficient in several wind instruments and continuously showered the audience with his mega-watt smile. Beware, there are lots of cheese-filled moments of friendly banter. We did, however, enjoy Az’s musical insights. The care and consideration given to the acoustics clearly show and added tremendously to the enjoyment of the musical performance. Furthermore, the audience, who was there to appreciate the music rather than just their drinks and dates, further enhanced our evening. The staff was effective in letting those who did not understand the basic courtesy of being respectful during a performance know their place. (A shout-out to MJ for noticing the displeasure of the general audience and shush-ing a group of loud-mouthed, wine-guzzling foreigners.) The music and the service alone were well worth the cover charge. However, perhaps ending the evening with a Bossa Nova piece or Viv’s rendition of “What a wonderful world” would have been more memorable. Aside from that, the only quibble was my strawberry daiquiri. Tasting more like a Shirley Temple, it was a bit of letdown to what could been a perfect evening.
label: positive

text: Well, I guess I now know why someone came up with the phrase 'Don't hold your breath'.The service was just okay. To say that it was super fantastic, then no, I'm sorry, but it wasn't.I liked the whole dark atmosphere, because it felt cosy. The only problem, for me, was that it was just way too crowded. No, not with people; it was crowded with, literally, junk. The place is already small enough; did they have to make it even worse by filling the place up with junk?I went with my family and friends, and our food took about forever and a day to arrive, and when it did, it was terribly disappointing. My lamb shank looked about the same size as my (then) skinny kitten's leg. I thought that maybe mine was the only small one, but it wasn't; my dad's and my friend's had skinny lamb shanks, too. I've had lamb elsewhere, and theirs were so much bigger, and so much more tender. The one in The Junk, though, was just so... blah. My mum's risotto looked so yummy, but tasted weird. My sister's dish, though, was humongous. What, fisherman's friend? Something like that. Anyway, I thought that it was worth the price, until I realised that it was only just topped with seafood, on top of a mountain of french fries, like sprinkles. When I realised that, I wanted to laugh, so loud, but I controlled myself. Needless to say, I don't think the food was worth it at all. Based on the prices, I expected it to, at least, taste good, but it didn't. If you're going to serve food like this, at least price it UNDER RM20, because that's all it's worth.And did I mention I hate the surroundings? The placement of JUNK throughout the place? Yeah, the place is called Junk, but you don't literally have to live up to the name and make it hard for everyone to find their footing when they walk around. Horrible!NOTE : One last thing; I know this only happened to me, but bear in mind that this could also happen to you.I had an iPod Touch, and I brought it with me to dinner here. After dinner, I accidentally left it at the table. I came back within five minute of leaving the place, when I realised that I'd misplaced my iPod. When I went back to my table, I talked to this one waiter, and asked him if he saw my iPod. He said that he didn't, and that he was the one who cleared the table. He looked extremely nervous, was fidgety, and was making a huge show that he didn't see it. He started pulling out chairs, looking under place mats, and almost got down to his knees to prove that it wasn't there. If the lighting had been better, I knew he'd have been sweating bullets. He even told me to search the trash can, because that's where HE threw whatever from the table when he cleaned up, and not long after he said that, he ran away from me as fast as he could. I can read people very well, and I tend to notice facial tics. The waiter? Guilty and nervous, he was trying to hide something. He tried to mislead me into thinking it was in one place, when, really, it was with him. I hate that, but I let him have my iPod. I can't do anything to him, so instead I'll just let the guilt literally kill him slowly, over time.Suffice to say, this place was terrible. I don't see why most everyone loves this place. It's overpriced, the food is disgusting and not even filling(even though we have to pay through our teeth for it), the employees are bloody dishonest and the place is way too littered with, I can't stress this enough, JUNK! You want a nice place to go for the evening? Don't consider going here. You're better off eating at Malay hawker stalls across river; at least their food is nice. Not to mention quite reasonably priced.
label: negative

text: history buffs know that James Brooke was the White Rajah but a few may be surprised to find a James Brooke Bistro smack in the middle of Kuching. Staying in Hilton, we spied from above this quaint detached house which we found out was a restaurant. so we decided to check it out by walking along the waterfront. with half a dozen frozen cats in their statuesque beauty serving as a sort of mascot outside, we saw a beautiful restaurant devoid of aircon that stood alone and aloof in the garden. venturing inside, we notice wood interior adorned with Sarawak motifs. ordered three fish dishes: two dory and one we could not recall that was fried as fish and chips. all three plates were well presented with salad and dip sauce. sharing all three a la musical chairs, my family concluded that it was pedestrian and not as alluring as made out by its menu which came with catchy titles and a bit of history of each dish. hope the chef could improve the food as the location was fantastic especially for tourists hoping to share a bit of Sarawakian history while enjoying their food.

text: Went for a birthday celebration and turned out on time for the reservation. We were immediately greeted by one of the chefs who took us a for a quick kitchen tour and explained to us how the restaurant concept was, including their efforts to reduce wastage leading to their decisions to use the extra products to make spreads or produce.We were seated promptly, and each course was brought out and explained to us. We had a total of sixteen or seventeen courses, which we included a juice pairing with. I’m not the most adventurous food person but I found each course absolutely delicious - I only refrained from having one of the desserts because I don’t take durian. The service, the quality of the food and the plating were impeccable.My husband has been to a few Michelin star restaurants and believes that the experience was comparable. Would recommend it if you love food and are adventurous - for those who aren’t it might not be the best option.
label: positive

text: We went for the New Year a Day Sunday a Brunch and had expected a great feast being a New Year. We were not disappointed and indeed lots of food that I had to strategise my priorities. The roast tempted me and that was a whole leg of beef. It looked very dry and I asked for a fatty cut with burnt crust. There was a separate piece probably not part of the leg that the chef cut for me. Fat was nice but the near was dry just like the leg, hence, a bit tough. Sashimi offerings are the usual cheap fare - butter fish, tuna, salmon and a white fish. Plain and not palatable. Then I next tried the bbq fare and they are reasonably good and well marinated. Smoked duck in cold platter was ordinary and we didn't finish. Mushroom soup was also ordinary and I had expected this to be great coming from a reputable restaurant in 5 Star Hotel - I had a few sips and gave up probably also detected some burnt smell. There are plenty of choices of cold crustaceans - impressive list made up of yabbies, Morton bay bugs type, prawns, crayfish, scallops, oysters and mussels. I picked one or two each and forced myself to finish so as not to waste food. Oysters despite my selections are tiny paper thin flesh. Crustaceans are cold and bland. Lastly to hope to end well we hit the desserts also with excellent selections - these saved rage day. In summary the presentation and deco got us all worked up and excited but when we started eating they failed miserably. Not the quality I expected if a much hyped establishment with quite a long history and experience. I will never come back to eat again.
label: neutral

text: I would like to prefix this review by saying that the actual food we had during our visit was good (if not great value), but what has really brought this rating down was the terrible service we experienced.Never ones to normally eat at the hotel where we are staying, my partner and I chose to visit Gobo Upstairs whilst staying at the Traders hotel due to the appeal their Australian Week presented. When we arrived at the restaurant (on a Tuesday night) there was just us, another couple and then what looked to be a work meeting involving 10-15 business men and women. Basically, not a busy restaurant in the slightest. We ordered drinks (a gin and tonic for myself and a beer for my partner) , with the plan being to get one of the Australian wines featured once those were finished. A waiter came over shortly after to take our food order, and we sat back to wait for our drinks. And waited. And waited.After about 10-15 minutes we got the attention of one of the floor staff, and inquired politely as to if our drinks were being prepared. I would like to point out at this point that my partner works in the service industry, and as a result we would normally give the benefit of the doubt for any delays, mistakes etc. as he can empathize with the staff. The staff member apologised, and said our drinks would be over shortly. Roughly about 10 minutes later our starters were delivered, but we were yet to receive our drinks. When the starters arrived we once again inquired as to where our drinks were, and were told, again, that they would be over shortly. In the end, they were not delivered until our starters had been cleared away. In the meantime, another family had arrived in the restaurant, been seated and served their drinks and starters whilst we were still waiting for ours. Putting a damper on the evening, we enjoyed our main course and would have liked to have had dessert - unfortunately, no staff ever offered it or brought us a dessert menu. After about 10 minutes of waiting (yet again), we'd had enough and decided to just get the bill and go elsewhere. Despite numerous staff going past our table, not one stopped to see if there was anything else we needed, or even glanced in our direction (most likely due to occupational embarrassment or not wanting to acknowledge the state of our evening's experience).In the end, my partner had to do the thing he hates people doing the most - standing up and waving his arms until someone came over. The bill was then delivered, although we insisted that the service charge be removed, due to lack of service we received (although this required us to complain to a variety of waiters at excruciating length until we spoke to the maitre d'). Reading about other customers experience around the time we visited really seems to highlight that although in a prominent location, Gobo Upstairs really does need to work on its customer service. I would not recommend this restaurant unless you were staying at Traders and desperate not to go out elsewhere.
label: negative
